Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine that I stuck to. I'd tell myself I'd study after my extracurriculars or on weekends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of the time I'd just open my laptop when I felt like it, scroll through some materials, and then get distracted. With 19 online courses, everything felt overwhelming and I couldn't figure out where to start, so often I just... didn't.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when I remembered a deadline was coming up. It wasn't really about regular times—it was more about when anxiety would hit me hard enough to actually do something. Sometimes I'd have a burst of energy and study for hours, but then I'd burn out and disappear again for a while.
Q3: Usually it was because I got a notification about something due soon, or I'd suddenly remember in the middle of the night that I hadn't checked the course in a while. Sometimes I logged in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety worse. The gamified tasks actually got me to log in more because there was something almost fun about them, even if I didn't always complete them.
Q4: There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit to check the LMS every morning. It worked for a bit when the gamified tasks gave me those little achievement badges—that actually felt kind of satisfying. But then life got busy with my other activities and I fell off. It never really became automatic for me the way I think it was supposed to.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could actually see what was being explained. I watched a lot of video content, like over two hours worth, because it felt easier than reading dense text. The gamified tasks were also engaging when I actually did them—they made learning feel less like a chore and more like a challenge I could tackle.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion posts and a lot of the reading materials. The discussions felt pointless because I didn't know anyone and felt awkward posting. The readings were just walls of text that I couldn't focus on, especially when I was already feeling anxious about being behind. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or get some kind of feedback right away. The gamified elements helped with that—earning points or completing a level made me feel like I accomplished something. If something was going to take forever with no clear payoff, I'd lose interest fast and move on to something else.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I knew they could help, but when I was already struggling to complete the required stuff, optional things felt like extra weight I couldn't carry. Required activities at least had deadlines that forced my hand. With optional stuff, there was no urgency, so my brain just filed them under 'maybe someday' and forgot about them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs to my phone so I could look at them during commutes or when I didn't have good internet at home—my resources are pretty limited. Sometimes I'd screenshot important diagrams and study from my camera roll. I also watched some YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials did.
Q10: There were times I'd be reviewing my downloaded notes or going through flashcards I made on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a friend from another class who explained things to me, which helped a lot but obviously the LMS had no idea that happened. My actual learning wasn't always happening inside the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video or document once, download it or take screenshots, and then never go back to the LMS version. It was easier to have everything saved locally where I could access it without logging in. My internet can be unreliable, so having offline copies felt necessary. The LMS probably thought I only looked at things once when really I reviewed them multiple times.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. I was juggling my extracurriculars, feeling super anxious about how behind I was, and just couldn't bring myself to face the LMS. The more I avoided it, the worse my anxiety got, which made me avoid it even more. It became this cycle where I knew I needed to engage but felt paralyzed every time I tried.
Q13: Absolutely. With limited resources at home and competing responsibilities, I couldn't always prioritize coursework the way I should have. When I was exhausted from other activities, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about what I was supposed to be doing made everything harder—I'd waste time trying to figure out where to start instead of actually studying.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, clicking through pages, watching videos on 2x speed, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I was going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. It was like my body was present but my mind had checked out because I was too anxious or tired to really focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety of starting something I didn't fully understand was worse than the anxiety of not doing it—at least in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd feel more prepared tomorrow, or that I worked better under pressure. By the time I finally started, it was usually too late to do a good job.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then frantically log in the night before something was due and try to cram. The problem was I'd often miss deadlines entirely because I lost track of them or couldn't bring myself to face them. My whole engagement was deadline-driven, which wasn't sustainable.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I didn't get much because I wasn't submitting enough work. The few times I did get comments, I felt surprised and kind of motivated to try harder. But then that feeling would fade and I'd fall back into old patterns. I think more regular, encouraging feedback might have helped me stay on track.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many courses and modules to keep track of. I never knew what to prioritize, and the sheer volume of content made me shut down. When the gamified tasks were introduced, that actually helped because it gave me smaller, more manageable goals. But overall, the course structure didn't match how my brain works—I needed more guidance and smaller steps.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier because they broke things into bite-sized pieces and gave me rewards for completing them. That worked with my visual learning style too. But the long reading assignments and the confusing navigation of the LMS made things harder. I'd get lost trying to find what I needed and give up out of frustration.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but feeling too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete anything. You might be watching videos, reading materials, genuinely trying to learn, but when it comes to submitting work, the anxiety or confusion stops you. High activity doesn't always mean you're in a good place to produce work.
Q21: That kind of describes me in some ways. My exam score wasn't terrible considering how little I actually did in the LMS—I think my offline studying and visual learning helped me retain some things. Some people just learn differently and don't need to be constantly logged in to absorb material. The LMS activity isn't the whole picture of what someone knows.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. It misses all the offline studying, the mental struggles, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging even when you want to. It's like looking at a shadow of what's really happening—you get the outline but not the full picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and wanting to understand the material. For me, engagement happened in bursts when something caught my interest, but sustaining it was the hard part. True engagement feels effortless; forced engagement feels exhausting.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything I did or didn't do. It wouldn't show the hours I spent worrying about the course instead of actually working on it, or the offline studying I did with downloaded materials. It also wouldn't show that I actually cared about learning—I just couldn't always show up the way the system expected me to. My struggle was invisible in the data.
